docs.text: Departures from the OTDF FIR inbound to OBBI, OBBS, and OBKH shall be transferred to Bahrain Approach (OBBI_APP) levelled at 12,000FT before PATOM, provided surrounding traffic is not a factor. Lower altitudes may be assigned only with coordination.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Modern ATC surveillance integrates multiple systems to provide accurate aircraft position data.

docs.title: Mode A
docs.text: • 4-digit squawk code

docs.title: Mode C
docs.text: • Pressure altitude

docs.title: Mode S
docs.text:
• Unique aircraft identification
  • Enhanced data capability

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• GPS-based position
  • High accuracy
  • No interrogation required

docs.title: 4. Multilateration (MLAT)
docs.text:
• Uses multiple ground stations
  • Calculates aircraft position via time difference

docs.title: 5. System Integration
docs.text:
Controllers must understand:

  • Data reliability differences
  • Update rates
  • Potential discrepancies between sources

docs.title: 1. Objective
docs.text: To positively identify an aircraft prior to providing radar control.

docs.title: 2.1 Squawk Identification
docs.text:
• Assign discrete code
  • Observe code change

docs.title: 2.2 IDENT Feature
docs.text:
• Pilot activates IDENT
  • Radar return highlights

docs.title: 2.3 Position Identification
docs.text: • Correlate reported position with radar return

docs.title: 2.4 Direct Recognition
docs.text:
• Known departure
  • Transfer from another controller

docs.title: 3. Verification Requirements
docs.text:
Controllers must confirm:

  • Callsign matches radar label
  • Position is consistent
  • No ambiguity exists

docs.title: 4. Loss of Identification
docs.text:
If identification is lost:

  • Inform pilot immediately
  • Re-establish identification
  • Apply procedural separation if required

docs.title: Lateral Separation
docs.text: • Standard: 5 NM

docs.title: Vertical Separation
docs.text:
• 1000 ft (RVSM airspace)
  • 2000 ft (non-RVSM where applicable)

docs.title: 2. Application
docs.text:
Controllers must:

  • Ensure continuous monitoring
  • Anticipate loss of separation
  • Apply corrective action early

docs.title: 3. Reduced Separation (if applicable)
docs.text:
• Subject to local SOPs
  • Requires surveillance system performance criteria

docs.title: 4. Wake Turbulence Considerations
docs.text: • Apply additional spacing where required

docs.title: 1. Radar Limitations
docs.text:
• Line-of-sight restrictions
  • Terrain shielding
  • Range limitations
  • Update delays

docs.title: 2. SSR Limitations
docs.text:
• Incorrect squawk selection
  • Altitude readout errors
  • Transponder failure

docs.title: 3. Human Factors
docs.text:
• Misidentification
  • Fixation
  • Loss of situational awareness

docs.title: 4. System Errors
docs.text:
• Label swaps
  • Track coasting
  • Data dropouts

docs.title: 5. Mitigation
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Cross-check data
  • Maintain situational awareness
  • Avoid over-reliance on automation

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To provide the controller with an understanding of airspace classes and the associated service requirements.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• ICAO airspace classes (A–G)
  • Separation requirements per class
  • VFR and IFR applicability
  • Service provision differences

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply appropriate separation based on airspace classification
  • Provide correct level of service to IFR and VFR traffic
  • Ensure compliance with controlled airspace requirements

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Airspace is classified to define the level of service and separation provided to aircraft.

docs.title: 2.1 Class A
docs.text:
• IFR only
  • Full ATC separation provided

docs.title: 2.2 Class B
docs.text:
• IFR and VFR
  • Separation provided to all aircraft

docs.title: 2.3 Class C
docs.text:
• IFR separated from IFR and VFR
  • VFR receives traffic information

docs.title: 2.4 Class D
docs.text:
• IFR separated from IFR
  • VFR receives traffic information

docs.title: 2.5 Class E
docs.text:
• IFR separated from IFR
  • Limited service to VFR

docs.title: 2.6 Class F & G
docs.text:
• Uncontrolled airspace
  • Advisory or information service only

docs.title: 2.7 Operational Implications
docs.text:
Controllers shall recognise:

  • In Class C airspace, IFR traffic is separated from both IFR and VFR traffic
  • In Class D airspace, VFR traffic may not be separated from other VFR aircraft
  • Traffic information shall be provided where separation is not applied

docs.title: 3.1 Controlled Airspace Operations
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall comply with ATC clearances
  • Separation shall be maintained by ATC

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• ICAO airspace classification rules apply
  • Separation requirements vary by class

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text:
• Most en-route operations occur in Class A or C
  • VATSIM environments typically simulate controlled airspace

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure understanding of airspace divisions and control responsibilities within an FIR/UIR structure.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Definition of FIR and UIR
  • ACC responsibilities
  • Boundaries and lateral limits
  • Vertical division of airspace

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Provide ATC service within assigned sector of the FIR/UIR
  • Coordinate with adjacent FIRs and sectors
  • Ensure correct transfer of control

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Airspace is divided into regions to define responsibility for air traffic services.

docs.title: 2.1 Flight Information Region (FIR)
docs.text:
• Defined area where flight information and alerting services are provided
  • Usually surface to upper limits

docs.title: 2.2 Upper Information Region (UIR)
docs.text:
• Upper portion of FIR
  • Typically above FL245 or as defined locally

docs.title: 2.3 Area Control Centre (ACC)
docs.text:
• Provides en-route ATC services
  • Responsible for separation within FIR/UIR

docs.title: 3.1 Transfer of Control
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall be transferred at agreed coordination points
  • Control responsibility shall be clearly defined

docs.title: 3.2 Coordination Principles
docs.text:
• Transfer of control and communication may occur at different points
  • Coordination shall be completed prior to boundary transfer
  • Estimates shall be passed in accordance with local procedures

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• FIR/UIR boundaries defined by ICAO and national authorities
  • Controllers shall not provide service outside designated airspace

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Adjacent FIR coordination is critical in high-density regions

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To enable the controller to understand and manage traffic along defined route structures.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• ATS route designators
  • Waypoints and navigation fixes
  • Airway structure
  • RNAV and direct routing

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue clearances consistent with ATS routes
  • Monitor aircraft adherence to cleared route
  • Apply direct routings where appropriate

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: ATS routes provide structured pathways for aircraft to follow within controlled airspace.

docs.title: 2.1 Airways
docs.text:
• Defined routes between waypoints
  • Identified by designators (e.g. UL602)

docs.title: 2.2 Waypoints
docs.text:
• Fixed geographical positions
  • Defined by coordinates or navigation aids

docs.title: 2.3 RNAV Operations
docs.text:
• Allows aircraft to fly direct between points
  • Reduces reliance on ground-based navigation

docs.title: 2.4 Free Route Airspace (FRA)
docs.text:
• Aircraft may plan direct routes between entry and exit points
  • Controllers must manage increased conflict complexity

docs.title: 2.5 Conditional Routes (CDR)
docs.text:
• Routes available based on activity (e.g. military airspace)
  • Availability may change dynamically

docs.title: 2.6 Traffic Flow Implications
docs.text:
Controllers shall recognise:

  • Converging ATS routes create predictable conflict points
  • Parallel routes may require monitoring for lateral deviations
  • Opposite-direction traffic on the same route requires strategic separation

docs.title: 3.1 Route Clearances
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall follow cleared route unless instructed otherwise
  • Direct routings may be issued to improve efficiency

docs.title: 3.2 Direct Routing
docs.text:
• Direct routings shall not compromise separation
  • Controllers shall assess downstream sector impact prior to issuing directs

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Routes shall comply with published AIP structure
  • Minimum separation shall be maintained regardless of routing

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Direct routing increases complexity in conflict detection

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure correct application of vertical separation and flight level allocation.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Flight level system
  • Transition altitude and level
  • RVSM airspace
  • Directional cruising levels

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Assign correct flight levels based on direction
  • Maintain vertical separation
  • Apply RVSM rules where applicable

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Flight levels are used to ensure vertical separation between aircraft.

docs.title: 2.1 Flight Levels
docs.text:
• Based on standard pressure (1013 hPa)
  • Expressed as FL (e.g. FL350)

docs.title: 2.2 Transition Altitude / Level
docs.text:
• Transition altitude: lowest usable flight level
  • Transition level: lowest available FL above TA

docs.title: 2.3 RVSM (Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum)
docs.text:
• Applies between FL290–FL410
  • Reduces vertical separation to 1000 ft

docs.title: 2.4 Directional Levels
docs.text:
• Eastbound vs westbound allocation
  • Based on magnetic track

docs.title: 3.1 Level Assignment
docs.text:
• Assign levels according to direction and traffic
  • Ensure no conflict with adjacent aircraft

docs.title: 3.2 Non-Standard Level Allocation
docs.text:
Controllers may assign non-standard levels:

  • To resolve conflicts
  • To manage traffic flow
  • To accommodate climb/descent profiles

docs.title: 3.3 Level Capping
docs.text:
Controllers may restrict climb:

  • To maintain separation
  • To integrate traffic into sector flow
  • Due adjacent sector constraints

docs.title: 3.4 Opposite Direction Traffic
docs.text:
• Standard level allocation may be overridden
  • Separation shall be ensured using alternative methods

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• 1000 ft separation in RVSM
  • 2000 ft outside RVSM (where applicable)

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Incorrect level assignment is a common cause of conflict

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To provide understanding of sector-based airspace division and workload management.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Sector boundaries
  • Traffic distribution
  • Controller workload principles
  • Dynamic sectorisation

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Operate within assigned sector limits
  • Maintain awareness of adjacent sectors
  • Manage workload effectively

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Airspace is divided into sectors to distribute workload and maintain safe operations.

docs.title: 2.1 Sector Boundaries
docs.text:
• Defined laterally and vertically
  • May be static or dynamic

docs.title: 2.2 Sector Capacity
docs.text:
• Maximum number of aircraft manageable safely
  • Influenced by complexity and traffic flow

docs.title: 2.3 Bandboxing
docs.text: • Combining multiple sectors under one controller

docs.title: 2.4 Complexity Factors
docs.text:
Sector workload is influenced by:

  • Crossing traffic
  • Climb and descent profiles
  • Speed differentials
  • Weather deviations

docs.title: 2.5 Tactical vs Strategic Workload
docs.text:
• Strategic workload: planning ahead (10–20 minutes)
  • Tactical workload: immediate control actions

Controllers shall balance both continuously.

docs.title: 2.6 Complexity vs Traffic Count
docs.text:
Workload is not determined solely by aircraft count.

Factors include:

  • Crossing traffic
  • Climb/descent conflicts
  • Speed variation
  • Weather deviations

docs.title: 3.1 Sector Handover
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall be transferred prior to boundary
  • Coordination shall be completed in advance

docs.title: 3.2 Sector Splitting
docs.text:
• Sectors may be split when workload exceeds safe limits
  • Additional controllers may be introduced

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Controllers shall not exceed manageable workload
  • Sector limits shall be strictly observed

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor sector awareness leads to coordination failures

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To define the core responsibilities of an Area Controller in a radar environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Legal and operational responsibility of ATC
  • Scope of control within a sector
  • Obligation to maintain separation
  • Duty of care

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Provide a safe, orderly and expeditious flow of traffic
  • Maintain separation at all times
  • Issue clear and timely instructions
  • Monitor aircraft continuously

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: The Area Controller is responsible for the safe and efficient management of all aircraft within their assigned sector.

docs.title: 2.1 Safety Responsibility
docs.text:
• Safety is the primary responsibility at all times
  • No operational decision shall compromise separation

docs.title: 2.2 Control Responsibility
docs.text:
• Applies to all identified aircraft within the sector
  • Includes monitoring, instruction, and intervention

docs.title: 2.3 Duty of Care
docs.text:
• Controllers shall act to prevent unsafe situations
  • Early intervention is required where risk is identified

docs.title: 3.1 Provision of Control
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall not be left unmanaged
  • Instructions shall be issued proactively

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Separation minima shall be maintained at all times
  • Controllers are accountable for instructions issued

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Passive control leads to conflict development

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To establish the correct priority order for controller actions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Safety vs efficiency
  • Task prioritisation
  • Workload management

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall prioritise:

  1. Separation
  2. Collision avoidance
  3. Traffic flow
  4. Efficiency

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Controllers must continuously prioritise tasks to ensure safety is never compromised.

docs.title: 2.1 Primary Priority: Separation
docs.text:
• Maintaining separation is the highest priority
  • All other tasks are secondary

docs.title: 2.2 Secondary Priority: Collision Avoidance
docs.text: • Immediate action required if separation is at risk

docs.title: 2.3 Traffic Flow
docs.text:
• Sequencing and spacing
  • Efficient routing

docs.title: 2.4 Efficiency
docs.text:
• Direct routings
  • Optimal levels

docs.title: 3.1 Task Management
docs.text:
• Controllers shall reassess priorities continuously
  • Lower priority tasks may be delayed if required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall never be compromised for efficiency

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • High workload requires strict prioritisation discipline

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To define ownership and application of separation.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• When ATC is responsible for separation
  • Transfer of responsibility
  • Pilot vs controller responsibility

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain separation between controlled aircraft
  • Ensure separation is transferred correctly
  • Monitor until responsibility is transferred

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Separation responsibility lies with the controller once radar control is established.

docs.title: 2.1 Controller Responsibility
docs.text:
• Applies to all identified aircraft under control
  • Continues until transfer of control

docs.title: 2.2 Transfer of Responsibility
docs.text:
• Occurs at defined coordination points
  • Must be clearly established

docs.title: 2.3 Pilot Responsibility
docs.text: • Applies in specific cases (e.g. visual separation if approved)

docs.title: 3.1 Handover
docs.text:
• Separation shall exist at the point of transfer
  • Receiving controller assumes responsibility upon acceptance

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Controllers shall not transfer conflicted traffic
  • Separation must be assured prior to transfer

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Assumptions during handover are a major risk factor

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To develop and maintain an accurate mental picture of traffic.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Traffic picture development
  • Projection and anticipation
  • Risk recognition

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain continuous awareness of all aircraft
  • Anticipate conflicts before they occur
  • Monitor trends, not just positions

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Situational awareness is the controller’s ability to understand current and future traffic situations.

docs.title: 2.1 Traffic Picture
docs.text: • Position, level, speed, intent

docs.title: 2.2 Projection
docs.text:
• Predict future positions
  • Identify conflicts early

docs.title: 2.3 Monitoring
docs.text:
• Continuous scan of sector
  • Avoid fixation

docs.title: 3.1 Scan Technique
docs.text:
• Systematic radar scan
  • Prioritise high-risk aircraft

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Conflicts should be identified early
  • Controllers shall not rely solely on system alerts

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Loss of awareness is a primary cause of incidents

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure effective and timely decision-making in a dynamic environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Tactical vs strategic decisions
  • Risk assessment
  • Intervention timing

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Make timely decisions to resolve conflicts
  • Apply the safest and most efficient solution
  • Avoid unnecessary complexity

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Decision-making in ATC involves selecting safe and effective actions under time pressure.

docs.title: 2.1 Tactical Decisions
docs.text:
• Immediate actions
  • Conflict resolution

docs.title: 2.2 Strategic Decisions
docs.text:
• Planning ahead
  • Traffic flow management

docs.title: 2.3 Risk Assessment
docs.text:
• Evaluate consequences
  • Choose safest option

docs.title: 3.1 Intervention Timing
docs.text:
• Early intervention preferred
  • Avoid last-minute actions

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Decisions shall prioritise safety over efficiency
  • Solutions shall be clear and manageable

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Late decisions increase workload and risk

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To provide an understanding of how traffic flows through an ACC sector and how controllers influence that flow.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Traffic flow vs individual control
  • Predictability and stability
  • Strategic vs tactical control

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain a stable and predictable traffic flow
  • Anticipate traffic patterns and conflicts
  • Apply control measures to prevent disruption

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Traffic flow refers to the movement of multiple aircraft through a sector in a structured and manageable manner.

docs.title: 2.1 Flow Stability
docs.text:
• Stable flows reduce workload
  • Unstable flows increase complexity

docs.title: 2.2 Predictability
docs.text:
• Predictable aircraft behaviour improves planning
  • Sudden changes increase risk

docs.title: 2.3 Strategic vs Tactical Control
docs.text:
• Strategic: planning ahead (10–20 minutes)
  • Tactical: immediate actions

docs.title: 3.1 Flow Management
docs.text:
• Controllers shall manage flows, not just individual aircraft
  • Early action shall be taken to prevent congestion

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Traffic flow shall remain manageable at all times

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Reactive control leads to instability

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure correct sequencing and spacing of aircraft in an en-route environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Longitudinal spacing
  • Speed control
  • Sequencing techniques

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Establish and maintain appropriate spacing between aircraft
  • Sequence traffic to avoid conflicts
  • Use speed and level adjustments effectively

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Sequencing ensures aircraft follow an orderly flow, while spacing maintains safe separation and efficiency.

docs.title: 2.1 Longitudinal Spacing
docs.text:
• Based on speed and distance
  • Prevents overtaking conflicts

docs.title: 2.2 Speed Control
docs.text:
• Primary tool for fine adjustments
  • Reduces need for vectoring

docs.title: 2.3 Level Management
docs.text: • Vertical separation used to support sequencing

docs.title: 3.1 Speed Instructions
docs.text:
• Shall be clear and achievable
  • Shall consider aircraft performance

docs.title: 3.2 Sequencing Strategy
docs.text: • Faster aircraft shall not be placed behind slower traffic without spacing

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Separation minima shall not be infringed
  • Spacing shall be sufficient to prevent conflict

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor sequencing leads to unnecessary workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To understand how traffic demand interacts with sector capacity.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Sector capacity limits
  • Traffic demand patterns
  • Complexity vs aircraft count

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Monitor sector capacity continuously
  • Recognise when demand exceeds manageable limits
  • Apply measures to reduce complexity

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Capacity refers to the maximum traffic a sector can handle safely, while demand is the number of aircraft requiring service.

docs.title: 2.1 Sector Capacity
docs.text:
• Defined by workload, not just aircraft count
  • Varies with complexity

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Demand
docs.text:
• Peaks and flows vary throughout the day
  • May exceed capacity if unmanaged

docs.title: 2.3 Complexity Factors
docs.text:
• Crossing routes
  • Climb/descent traffic
  • Speed variation

docs.title: 3.1 Monitoring
docs.text:
• Controllers shall anticipate capacity issues
  • Action shall be taken before overload occurs

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Sector workload shall remain within safe limits

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • High traffic does not always mean high complexity

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply techniques that regulate traffic flow and reduce complexity.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Speed control
  • Vectoring
  • Level restrictions
  • Direct routings

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use appropriate techniques to regulate traffic flow
  • Apply minimal intervention required to maintain control

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Flow control measures are used to maintain safe and efficient traffic movement.

docs.title: 2.1 Speed Control
docs.text: • Primary tool for minor adjustments

docs.title: 2.2 Vectoring
docs.text: • Used to resolve conflicts or manage spacing

docs.title: 2.3 Level Restrictions
docs.text: • Prevent conflicts and manage flow

docs.title: 2.4 Direct Routing
docs.text: • Improves efficiency but may increase complexity

docs.title: 3.1 Application of Measures
docs.text:
• Controllers shall select the simplest effective solution
  • Actions shall not create secondary conflicts

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Safety shall not be compromised for efficiency

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Overuse of vectoring increases workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To identify and manage traffic bottlenecks and complexity within a sector.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Bottleneck locations
  • Conflict hotspots
  • Complexity drivers

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Identify high-risk areas within the sector
  • Apply proactive measures to reduce complexity
  • Manage traffic before bottlenecks form

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Bottlenecks occur where traffic converges, increasing the risk of conflict and workload.

docs.title: 2.1 Bottlenecks
docs.text:
• Entry/exit points
  • Crossing routes
  • Level-off points

docs.title: 2.2 Complexity Drivers
docs.text:
• High traffic density
  • Mixed climb/descent profiles
  • Weather deviations

docs.title: 2.3 Conflict Hotspots
docs.text:
• Predictable areas of risk
  • Require increased attention

docs.title: 3.1 Complexity Management
docs.text:
• Early intervention shall be applied
  • Traffic shall be organised before reaching bottlenecks

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall maintain manageable workload at all times

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Late action leads to sector overload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To provide the fundamental principles governing the use of radar vectoring in an en-route environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Purpose of vectoring
  • When to vector vs when not to
  • Impact on traffic flow

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use vectoring only when necessary
  • Apply minimal deviation from cleared route
  • Maintain predictable aircraft behaviour

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vectoring is the provision of heading instructions to aircraft to achieve a desired track or position.

docs.title: 2.1 Purpose of Vectoring
docs.text:
• Conflict resolution
  • Traffic sequencing
  • Positioning for climb/descent

docs.title: 2.2 Predictability
docs.text:
• Excessive or unnecessary vectoring increases complexity
  • Predictable tracks reduce workload

docs.title: 2.3 Minimal Intervention
docs.text: • Small corrections are preferred over large deviations

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Vectoring shall be used when required to maintain safety or efficiency
  • Aircraft shall be returned to route as soon as practicable

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Separation shall be maintained at all times
  • Vectoring shall not create additional conflicts

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Over-vectoring is a common controller error

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure correct and standardised use of heading instructions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Heading phraseology
  • Magnetic headings
  • Instruction clarity

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue clear and concise heading instructions
  • Ensure instructions are unambiguous
  • Monitor compliance

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Heading instructions are used to direct aircraft along a specified track.

docs.title: 2.1 Magnetic Headings
docs.text:
• Expressed in degrees
  • Three-digit format (e.g. 090, 270)

docs.title: 2.2 Types of Instructions
docs.text:
• “Turn left heading XXX”
  • “Turn right heading XXX”
  • “Fly heading XXX”

docs.title: 3.1 Issuing Headings
docs.text:
• Instructions shall include direction and heading where applicable
  • Avoid ambiguous instructions

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Phraseology shall comply with ICAO standards

docs.title: Examples:
docs.text:
• "Turn left heading 270"
  • "Fly heading 180"
  • "Turn right heading 090"

docs.title: 6. Notes
docs.text: • Late or unclear instructions increase pilot workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To understand the geometry of vectoring and how to achieve accurate intercepts.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Intercept angles
  • Closure rates
  • Relative positioning

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply appropriate intercept angles
  • Avoid overshoot
  • Position aircraft efficiently

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vectoring requires an understanding of relative geometry between aircraft and desired tracks.

docs.title: 2.1 Intercept Angles
docs.text:
• Small angles (20–30°) for gentle intercepts
  • Larger angles for rapid positioning

docs.title: 2.2 Closure Rate
docs.text:
• Depends on speed and angle
  • High closure requires early action

docs.title: 2.3 Overshoot Prevention
docs.text: • Reduce intercept angle as aircraft approaches track

docs.title: 3.1 Intercept Technique
docs.text:
• Assign initial intercept heading
  • Adjust as required to capture track

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Intercepts shall be smooth and controlled

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor geometry leads to repeated corrections

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply vectoring techniques to maintain or restore separation.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Lateral separation through headings
  • Conflict geometry
  • Timing of intervention

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use vectoring to resolve conflicts early
  • Apply sufficient heading change to ensure separation
  • Monitor until conflict is resolved

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vectoring is a primary method for resolving conflicts in radar environments.

docs.title: 2.1 Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
• Heading changes alter aircraft track
  • Separation is achieved through divergence

docs.title: 2.2 Timing
docs.text:
• Early action reduces required deviation
  • Late action increases workload and risk

docs.title: 2.3 Magnitude of Turn
docs.text:
• Must be sufficient to ensure separation
  • Minor corrections may be ineffective

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Identify conflict
  • Issue heading instruction
  • Monitor until separation assured

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation minima shall not be infringed

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Insufficient turns are a common cause of loss of separation

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To use vectoring to improve traffic efficiency without compromising safety.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Direct routings vs vectoring
  • Track shortening
  • Flow improvement

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply vectoring to reduce track miles where appropriate
  • Balance efficiency with workload and complexity

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vectoring may be used to improve efficiency, but must be applied carefully.

docs.title: 2.1 Track Reduction
docs.text:
• Shorter routes reduce flight time
  • May increase complexity if overused

docs.title: 2.2 Flow Improvement
docs.text: • Adjust positioning to improve sequencing

docs.title: 2.3 Risk Considerations
docs.text: • Efficiency shall not compromise separation

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Assess impact before issuing vectors
  • Avoid unnecessary complexity

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Safety takes priority over efficiency

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Over-optimisation increases workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To establish the principles governing vertical control in an en-route environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Vertical separation minima
  • Climb and descent profiles
  • Interaction with lateral control

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain vertical separation at all times
  • Anticipate level changes and conflicts
  • Integrate vertical and lateral strategies

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vertical control involves managing aircraft altitude to ensure separation and support traffic flow.

docs.title: 2.1 Vertical Separation
docs.text:
• Standard separation: 1000 ft (RVSM)
  • Increased separation outside RVSM where applicable

docs.title: 2.2 Vertical Profiles
docs.text:
• Aircraft climb and descent rates vary
  • Performance affects conflict timing

docs.title: 2.3 Integration with Lateral Control
docs.text: • Vertical and lateral solutions may be combined

docs.title: 3.1 Monitoring
docs.text:
• Controllers shall monitor climb and descent continuously
  • Deviations shall be identified early

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Vertical separation shall not be infringed

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Vertical conflicts develop quickly if unmanaged

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage aircraft climbs safely and efficiently within a controlled environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Climb rates
  • Level allocation
  • Traffic integration

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue climb clearances in accordance with traffic conditions
  • Ensure no conflict with aircraft above
  • Monitor climb progress

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Climb management ensures aircraft reach cruising levels without compromising separation.

docs.title: 2.1 Climb Profiles
docs.text:
• Aircraft climb rates vary by type and weight
  • Rate reduces at higher levels

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Above
docs.text: • Aircraft above must be considered before issuing climb

docs.title: 2.3 Step Climbs
docs.text: • Used when immediate climb is not possible

docs.title: 3.1 Climb Clearance
docs.text:
• "Climb FLXXX"
  • May include restrictions if required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall be maintained throughout climb

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Unmonitored climbs can lead to vertical conflict

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage aircraft descents in a safe and controlled manner.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Descent profiles
  • Timing of descent
  • Interaction with lower traffic

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue descent instructions at appropriate time
  • Ensure no conflict with aircraft below
  • Sequence descending traffic effectively

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Descent management involves controlling when and how aircraft descend to lower levels.

docs.title: 2.1 Descent Profiles
docs.text:
• Descent rates are generally higher than climb rates
  • Aircraft may require early descent planning

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Below
docs.text: • Aircraft below must be protected at all times

docs.title: 2.3 Continuous Descent
docs.text: • Preferred for efficiency where possible

docs.title: 3.1 Descent Clearance
docs.text:
• "Descend FLXXX"
  • May include restrictions

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall be maintained throughout descent

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Late descent creates compression and workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To safely manage aircraft whose vertical profiles intersect.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Crossing conflict geometry
  • Timing of level changes
  • Vertical vs lateral solutions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Identify crossing conflicts early
  • Apply vertical or lateral solutions as appropriate
  • Monitor until conflict is resolved

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Crossing traffic presents a high risk of conflict when aircraft climb or descend through occupied levels.

docs.title: 2.1 Conflict Geometry
docs.text: • Occurs when aircraft paths intersect vertically and laterally

docs.title: 2.2 Timing
docs.text: • Early action reduces required intervention

docs.title: 2.3 Solution Options
docs.text:
• Stop climb/descent
  • Assign alternative level
  • Apply vectoring

docs.title: 3.1 Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
• Issue level restriction or alternative clearance
  • Monitor until separation assured

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall not be compromised during level change

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Crossing conflicts are a common source of error

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply level restrictions and plan vertical profiles effectively.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Level capping
  • Traffic planning
  • Integration with flow management

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply level restrictions to manage traffic
  • Plan climbs and descents in advance
  • Prevent future conflicts through early planning

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Level planning is used to organise traffic vertically and reduce complexity.

docs.title: 2.1 Level Capping
docs.text:
• Restrict climb or descent temporarily
  • Used to maintain separation

docs.title: 2.2 Vertical Planning
docs.text:
• Plan ahead based on traffic flow
  • Avoid reactive control

docs.title: 2.3 Flow Integration
docs.text: • Align vertical profiles with sector traffic

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Restrictions shall be clear and achievable
  • Adjustments shall be made as required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall maintain a structured vertical profile

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor planning leads to last-minute conflict resolution

docs.title: 7.1 General
docs.text:
Conflict resolution ensures that minimum separation standards are maintained between aircraft at all times.

Area controllers must manage conflicts across: - Large volumes of airspace - High-speed aircraft - Multiple flight levels

docs.title: Key Objective
docs.text: Maintain separation while minimising disruption to traffic flow.

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To develop the controller’s ability to detect potential conflicts early through effective monitoring and traffic analysis.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Visual scanning techniques
  • Trend analysis and projection
  • Relative speed and track assessment
  • Vertical profile monitoring

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Continuously scan the radar display
  • Identify converging tracks and potential conflicts
  • Anticipate future aircraft positions
  • Detect conflicts at the earliest stage

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
Conflict detection is the process of identifying situations where separation minima may be infringed.

Early detection allows for simple and effective resolution.

docs.title: 2.1 Visual Scanning
docs.text:
• Continuous monitoring of radar display
  • Identification of converging tracks
  • Prioritisation of high-risk aircraft

docs.title: 2.2 Trend Analysis
docs.text:
• Assessment of relative speed and heading
  • Prediction of future aircraft positions
  • Identification of closing distances

docs.title: 2.3 Level Awareness
docs.text:
• Monitoring climb and descent profiles
  • Identification of potential vertical conflicts
  • Awareness of level-offs and restrictions

docs.title: 3.1 Continuous Monitoring
docs.text:
• Controllers shall maintain a systematic radar scan
  • High-risk situations shall be prioritised

docs.title: 3.2 Early Identification
docs.text:
• Conflicts shall be identified sufficiently early to allow simple resolution
  • Late detection shall be avoided

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Conflict detection shall occur prior to loss of separation
  • Controllers shall not rely solely on system alerts

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text:
• Fixation on a single aircraft reduces detection capability
  • Poor scanning technique leads to late conflict identification

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To identify and understand the different types of conflicts encountered in an en-route radar environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Conflict geometry
  • Relative track and speed
  • Vertical vs lateral conflict types
  • Risk characteristics of each conflict type

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Identify the type of conflict at an early stage
  • Select appropriate resolution methods based on conflict geometry
  • Anticipate how different conflict types develop over time

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
Conflicts occur when two or more aircraft are predicted to infringe separation minima.

Understanding the type of conflict allows the controller to apply the most effective resolution.

docs.title: 2.1 Crossing Traffic
docs.text:
Aircraft on intersecting routes.

  • Typically occurs at fixes or airway intersections
  • Closure rate depends on relative angles
  • Requires early assessment of crossing point

docs.title: 2.2 Opposite Direction
docs.text:
Aircraft on reciprocal or near-reciprocal tracks.

  • High closure rate
  • Requires early and decisive intervention
  • Often resolved using vertical separation or large heading change

docs.title: 2.3 Overtaking
docs.text:
Faster aircraft catching slower traffic on similar tracks.

  • Closure rate may be gradual
  • Often requires speed control or level change
  • Can be overlooked if monitoring is poor

docs.title: 2.4 Vertical Conflict
docs.text:
Aircraft climbing or descending through occupied levels.

  • Common during climb/descent phases
  • Requires monitoring of vertical profiles
  • Timing is critical to avoid loss of separation

docs.title: 3.1 Conflict Identification
docs.text:
• Controllers shall assess relative track, speed, and level
  • Conflict type shall be determined prior to intervention

docs.title: 3.2 Resolution Planning
docs.text:
• Resolution shall be appropriate to conflict type
  • Early action shall be taken where required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Controllers shall correctly identify conflict type prior to issuing instructions
  • Resolution shall ensure separation is maintained at all times

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text:
• Opposite direction conflicts present the highest risk due to closure rate
  • Crossing conflicts are the most common in structured airspace
  • Vertical conflicts frequently occur due to poor climb/descent monitoring

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply appropriate techniques to resolve conflicts safely and efficiently.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Lateral resolution
  • Vertical resolution
  • Speed control

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Select the most effective resolution method
  • Apply minimum required intervention
  • Avoid creating secondary conflicts

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Conflict resolution involves altering aircraft trajectory to maintain separation.

docs.title: 2.1 Lateral Resolution
docs.text:
• Heading changes
  • Effective for most conflict types

docs.title: 2.2 Vertical Resolution
docs.text:
• Level changes
  • Useful for opposite direction and crossing conflicts

docs.title: 2.3 Speed Control
docs.text:
• Fine adjustments
  • Supports sequencing and spacing

docs.title: 3.1 Selection of Technique
docs.text:
• Based on conflict type and geometry
  • Consider downstream impact

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Resolution shall ensure separation is maintained

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Overcomplicated solutions increase workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To distinguish between strategic and tactical conflict management.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Planning vs immediate action
  • Traffic flow integration

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use strategic planning to prevent conflicts
  • Apply tactical action when required

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Conflict management occurs at two levels: strategic and tactical.

docs.title: 2.1 Strategic Control
docs.text:
• Planning ahead (10–20 minutes)
  • Reduces future conflicts

docs.title: 2.2 Tactical Control
docs.text:
• Immediate intervention
  • Resolves active conflicts

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Strategic control preferred where possible
  • Tactical control used when required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall not rely solely on tactical intervention

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Excessive tactical control increases workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure correct timing of conflict resolution actions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Early vs late intervention
  • Closure rates
  • Decision timing

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Intervene early to simplify resolution
  • Avoid last-minute actions

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Timing is critical in conflict resolution.

docs.title: 2.1 Early Intervention
docs.text:
• Requires smaller corrections
  • Reduces workload

docs.title: 2.2 Late Intervention
docs.text:
• Requires larger deviations
  • Increases risk

docs.title: 3.1 Decision Timing
docs.text: • Controllers shall act before conflict becomes critical

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Interventions shall be timely and effective

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Late decisions are a common cause of loss of separation

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage multiple simultaneous conflicts within a sector.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Prioritisation
  • Conflict interaction
  • Workload management

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Prioritise conflicts based on risk
  • Avoid resolving one conflict at the expense of another
  • Maintain overall situational awareness

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Multiple conflicts may occur simultaneously in high-density sectors.

docs.title: 2.1 Prioritisation
docs.text: • Highest risk conflicts addressed first

docs.title: 2.2 Interaction
docs.text: • One resolution may create another conflict

docs.title: 3.1 Management
docs.text: • Controllers shall consider the entire traffic picture

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • All conflicts shall be resolved without loss of separation

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Tunnel vision is a major risk in multi-conflict scenarios

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To identify common errors in conflict detection and resolution.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Typical controller mistakes
  • Risk factors

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Recognise and avoid common errors
  • Maintain disciplined control techniques

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Errors in conflict management can lead to loss of separation.

docs.title: 2.1 Late Detection
docs.text: • Failure to identify conflicts early

docs.title: 2.2 Insufficient Action
docs.text: • Heading changes too small

docs.title: 2.3 Fixation
docs.text: • Focusing on one aircraft

docs.title: 2.4 Over-Control
docs.text: • Unnecessary complexity

docs.title: 3. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall apply disciplined and effective control

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text: • Most errors are linked to poor situational awareness

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To reinforce effective conflict management techniques.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Efficient control techniques
  • Workload management

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply simple and effective solutions
  • Maintain situational awareness
  • Plan ahead

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Best practice ensures safe and efficient operations.

docs.title: 2.1 Simplicity
docs.text: • Use the simplest effective solution

docs.title: 2.2 Planning
docs.text: • Anticipate future conflicts

docs.title: 2.3 Awareness
docs.text: • Maintain full traffic picture

docs.title: 3. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall operate proactively and efficiently

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text: • Good control is proactive, not reactive

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To understand the purpose and application of holding in an en-route environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Purpose of holding
  • When to apply holding
  • Impact on traffic flow

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use holding to regulate traffic flow
  • Apply holding when demand exceeds capacity
  • Maintain separation within the hold

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Holding is used to delay aircraft in a controlled manner when traffic demand exceeds available capacity.

docs.title: 2.1 Purpose of Holding
docs.text:
• Delay absorption
  • Traffic sequencing
  • Flow regulation

docs.title: 2.2 When to Use Holding
docs.text:
• Sector overload
  • Arrival congestion
  • Downstream restrictions

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Holding shall be applied before congestion becomes unmanageable
  • Aircraft shall be issued clear holding instructions

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall be maintained at all times within the hold

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Late use of holding leads to instability

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply standard ICAO holding procedures.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Holding pattern structure
  • Inbound and outbound legs
  • Timing and speed considerations

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue holding clearances correctly
  • Ensure aircraft comply with published procedures

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: A standard holding pattern consists of racetrack-shaped tracks defined by inbound course and turns.

docs.title: 2.1 Holding Pattern
docs.text:
• Inbound leg
  • Outbound leg
  • Standard rate turns

docs.title: 2.2 Direction of Turns
docs.text:
• Standard: right turns
  • Non-standard: left turns as published

docs.title: 2.3 Timing
docs.text:
• Typically 1 minute inbound below FL140
  • 1.5 minutes above FL140

docs.title: 3.1 Holding Clearance
docs.text:
• Fix
  • Level
  • Inbound track
  • Turn direction
  • Timing if required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • ICAO holding procedures shall be applied

docs.title: Examples:
docs.text: • "Hold at [fix], maintain FLXXX"

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To understand standard holding entry techniques.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Direct entry
  • Teardrop entry
  • Parallel entry

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Understand entry techniques used by pilots
  • Anticipate aircraft positioning during entry

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Aircraft may enter a holding pattern using different techniques depending on their inbound track.

docs.title: 2.1 Direct Entry
docs.text:
• Simplest entry
  • Aircraft joins holding pattern directly

docs.title: 2.2 Teardrop Entry
docs.text: • Offset entry to intercept inbound track

docs.title: 2.3 Parallel Entry
docs.text: • Aircraft flies outbound before turning back

docs.title: 3.1 Controller Consideration
docs.text: • Controllers shall anticipate track deviations during entry

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Entry technique is pilot responsibility

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Entry phase increases complexity

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage multiple aircraft within a holding stack.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Vertical stacking
  • Separation within holds
  • Sequencing aircraft

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Assign levels to maintain separation
  • Sequence aircraft for exit
  • Monitor stack stability

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Holding stacks are used to manage multiple aircraft at a single fix.

docs.title: 2.1 Vertical Separation
docs.text:
• Aircraft separated by level
  • Standard vertical minima apply

docs.title: 2.2 Sequencing
docs.text:
• Aircraft exit hold in planned order
  • Lower aircraft typically released first

docs.title: 2.3 Stability
docs.text: • Stable stack reduces workload

docs.title: 3.1 Stack Management
docs.text:
• Controllers shall maintain organised levels
  • Adjust levels as required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall be maintained within the stack

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor stack management leads to congestion

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply methods to absorb delay without excessive use of holding.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Speed control
  • Vectoring for delay
  • Level restrictions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use appropriate techniques to absorb delay
  • Minimise use of holding where possible

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Delay can be absorbed using multiple techniques, not just holding.

docs.title: 2.1 Speed Control
docs.text: • Reduce speed to increase spacing

docs.title: 2.2 Vectoring
docs.text: • Extend track distance

docs.title: 2.3 Level Restrictions
docs.text: • Delay climb or descent

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall select the least disruptive method

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Safety shall not be compromised

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Holding is a last resort in many situations

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To introduce the principles of coordination between controllers and units.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Coordination requirements
  • Responsibility transfer
  • Communication principles

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Coordinate all relevant information prior to transfer
  • Ensure clarity of responsibility at all times
  • Avoid assumptions between units

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Coordination ensures the safe and orderly transfer of aircraft between controllers and units.

docs.title: 2.1 Purpose of Coordination
docs.text:
• Maintain separation
  • Ensure continuity of control
  • Support efficient traffic flow

docs.title: 2.2 Responsibility
docs.text:
• The transferring controller remains responsible until transfer is complete
  • The receiving controller assumes responsibility upon acceptance

docs.title: 3. Standards
docs.text: • Coordination shall be clear, complete, and timely

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text: • Lack of coordination is a major cause of operational error

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure safe and efficient transfer of aircraft between sectors within an ACC.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Transfer of control vs communication
  • Coordination requirements
  • Standard transfer conditions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Transfer aircraft in a coordinated and stable condition
  • Ensure no conflicts exist at the boundary
  • Comply with agreed sector procedures

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Sector to sector coordination ensures the safe and orderly transfer of aircraft between controllers within the same ACC.

docs.title: 2.1 Internal ACC Transfers
docs.text:
Aircraft shall be transferred between sectors:

  • At agreed levels
  • On agreed routing
  • With no outstanding conflicts

docs.title: 2.2 Transfer of Control
docs.text:
• Responsibility transfers at defined coordination point
  • Receiving controller assumes separation responsibility upon acceptance

docs.title: 2.3 Transfer of Communication
docs.text:
• May occur before or after transfer of control
  • Shall be clearly understood between controllers

docs.title: 3.1 Transferring Controller
docs.text:
• Resolve conflicts prior to transfer
  • Ensure aircraft is stable (level, speed, route)
  • Coordinate any non-standard conditions

docs.title: 3.2 Receiving Controller
docs.text:
• Accept aircraft only when able to provide separation
  • Clarify any uncertainty prior to acceptance

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall not be transferred in conflict
  • Coordination shall be completed prior to boundary

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text:
• Poor coordination is a major cause of loss of separation
  • Assumptions between controllers increase risk

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To ensure effective coordination between en-route and approach control units.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Arrival and departure coordination
  • Level restrictions
  • Transfer conditions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Coordinate aircraft in accordance with agreed procedures
  • Deliver aircraft in a stable and expected condition
  • Comply with approach unit requirements

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: ACC and APP coordination ensures smooth transition between en-route and terminal environments.

docs.title: 2.1 Arrivals
docs.text:
• Aircraft shall be delivered:
  • At agreed level
  • On agreed routing
  • At appropriate speed

docs.title: 2.2 Departures
docs.text:
• ACC shall accept aircraft:
  • After departure release
  • In accordance with coordination agreements

docs.title: 3.1 Coordination Requirements
docs.text:
• Level, speed, and route shall be coordinated where required
  • Non-standard conditions shall be agreed prior to transfer

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Aircraft shall meet agreed transfer conditions

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Unstable arrivals increase workload for approach controllers

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To coordinate aircraft between different FIRs.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• FIR boundaries
  • Letters of Agreement (LOA)
  • Coordination points

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Coordinate aircraft in accordance with LOAs
  • Ensure compliance with boundary conditions
  • Maintain separation until transfer

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: External coordination ensures safe transfer between FIRs.

docs.title: 2.1 Coordination Points
docs.text:
• Defined boundary fixes
  • Transfer occurs at agreed points

docs.title: 2.2 LOA Compliance
docs.text:
• Defines:
  • Levels
  • Routes
  • Speeds

docs.title: 3.1 Coordination
docs.text:
• Controllers shall comply with LOA requirements
  • Deviations shall be coordinated in advance

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Aircraft shall meet agreed boundary conditions

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Failure to follow LOA leads to coordination breakdown

docs.title: 2.1 Coordination Points
docs.text:
• Defined boundary fixes
  • Transfer occurs at agreed points

docs.title: 2.2 LOA Compliance
docs.text:
• Defines:
  • Levels
  • Routes
  • Speeds

docs.title: 3.1 Coordination
docs.text:
• Controllers shall comply with LOA requirements
  • Deviations shall be coordinated in advance

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Aircraft shall meet agreed boundary conditions

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Failure to follow LOA leads to coordination breakdown

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To distinguish between transfer of control and transfer of communication.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Definitions of control and communication transfer
  • Timing differences

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Ensure clarity of responsibility at all times
  • Transfer communication appropriately

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Transfer of control and communication may occur at different points.

docs.title: 2.1 Transfer of Control
docs.text: • Responsibility for separation transfers

docs.title: 2.2 Transfer of Communication
docs.text: • Aircraft changes frequency

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Controllers shall clearly understand when responsibility transfers
  • Communication transfer shall not create ambiguity

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Responsibility shall always be clearly defined

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Confusion between control and communication is a common error

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply standard coordination agreements for levels and speeds.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Standard level agreements
  • Speed restrictions
  • Flow integration

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Comply with agreed levels and speeds
  • Coordinate deviations when required

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Level and speed agreements standardise coordination and reduce workload.

docs.title: 2.1 Level Agreements
docs.text: • Defined in LOAs or local procedures

docs.title: 2.2 Speed Control
docs.text: • Used to manage flow and spacing

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Controllers shall adhere to agreed parameters
  • Non-standard conditions shall be coordinated

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Agreements shall be followed unless otherwise coordinated

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Deviations without coordination create downstream conflicts

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage situations that fall outside standard agreements.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• When coordination is required
  • Communication clarity

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Coordinate all deviations from standard conditions
  • Ensure mutual understanding

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Non-standard coordination occurs when aircraft do not meet agreed conditions.

docs.title: 2.1 Examples
docs.text:
• Incorrect level
  • Off-route aircraft
  • Speed deviations

docs.title: 3.1 Coordination
docs.text:
• Controllers shall communicate clearly and explicitly
  • Agreement shall be confirmed

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Aircraft shall not be transferred without agreement

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Assumed coordination is unsafe

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To maintain effective coordination under high workload conditions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Workload management
  • Prioritisation

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain coordination standards under pressure
  • Prioritise safety-critical communication

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: High workload increases risk of coordination errors.

docs.title: 2.1 Prioritisation
docs.text: • Safety-critical coordination first

docs.title: 2.2 Simplification
docs.text: • Use standard agreements where possible

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall avoid unnecessary coordination complexity

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Coordination shall remain clear and effective

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Workload does not justify reduced safety standards

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To establish the principles of standard phraseology in an en-route environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• ICAO phraseology standards
  • Clarity and brevity
  • Readback requirements

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use standard ICAO phraseology at all times
  • Speak clearly and concisely
  • Avoid non-standard or informal language

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Standard phraseology ensures consistent and unambiguous communication between controllers and pilots.

docs.title: 2.1 Clarity
docs.text: • Instructions shall be clear and easily understood

docs.title: 2.2 Brevity
docs.text: • Communication shall be concise without loss of meaning

docs.title: 2.3 Standardisation
docs.text: • Only approved phraseology shall be used

docs.title: 3.1 Readback
docs.text:
• Controllers shall ensure correct readback of critical instructions:
  • Levels
  • Headings
  • Speeds
  • Clearances

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Non-standard phraseology shall be avoided

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Poor phraseology leads to misunderstanding and increased workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply standard phraseology for radar control services.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Identification phraseology
  • Radar service provision
  • Traffic information

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use correct phraseology when identifying aircraft
  • Provide radar services clearly
  • Issue traffic information where required

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Radar phraseology is used when providing surveillance-based services.

docs.title: 2.1 Identification
docs.text:
• "Radar contact"
  • "Identified"

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Information
docs.text: • "Traffic, [clock position], [distance], [level], [direction]"

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Phraseology shall match the service being provided

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall use ICAO standard terms only

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Incorrect phraseology can cause confusion in busy environments

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To issue clear and correct level and speed instructions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Level assignment
  • Speed control phraseology

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue level instructions clearly
  • Apply speed control effectively

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Level and speed instructions are critical for maintaining separation and managing flow.

docs.title: 2.1 Level Instructions
docs.text:
• "Climb FLXXX"
  • "Descend FLXXX"
  • "Maintain FLXXX"

docs.title: 2.2 Speed Instructions
docs.text:
• "Reduce speed XXX knots"
  • "Maintain XXX knots"
  • "Minimum clean speed"

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Instructions shall be unambiguous
  • Controllers shall confirm correct readback

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Phraseology shall comply with ICAO standards

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Incorrect level or speed instructions can lead to loss of separation

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To issue correct heading and navigation instructions.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Heading phraseology
  • Direct routing
  • Navigation instructions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Issue accurate heading instructions
  • Provide navigation guidance as required

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Vectoring and navigation instructions are used to control aircraft track and positioning.

docs.title: 2.1 Heading Instructions
docs.text:
• "Turn left heading XXX"
  • "Turn right heading XXX"
  • "Fly heading XXX"

docs.title: 2.2 Direct Routing
docs.text: • "Proceed direct [waypoint]"

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall ensure instructions are clear and achievable

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Phraseology shall remain standardised

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Ambiguous instructions increase pilot workload

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To apply correct phraseology during coordination between controllers.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Coordination terminology
  • Agreement confirmation

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use clear and concise coordination language
  • Confirm agreements explicitly

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Coordination phraseology ensures mutual understanding between controllers.

docs.title: 2.1 Standard Coordination
docs.text: • Level, route, speed confirmation

docs.title: 2.2 Agreement
docs.text: • Explicit confirmation required

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall avoid ambiguity during coordination

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Coordination shall be clear and confirmed

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Miscommunication between controllers increases risk

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage communication in abnormal or non-standard situations.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Plain language use
  • Emergency communication

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Use plain language when required
  • Maintain clarity under abnormal conditions

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Non-standard situations may require deviation from standard phraseology.

docs.title: 2.1 Plain Language
docs.text: • Used when standard phraseology is insufficient

docs.title: 2.2 Emergency Situations
docs.text: • Clear and direct communication required

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall prioritise clarity over strict phraseology when required

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Communication shall remain clear and unambiguous

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Overuse of non-standard phraseology increases confusion

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To establish the principles for handling emergency and abnormal situations.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Controller priorities
  • Emergency handling principles
  • Decision-making under pressure

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Prioritise safety above all other considerations
  • Remain calm and act decisively
  • Provide maximum assistance to aircraft in difficulty

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Emergency situations require immediate attention and effective management to ensure safety.

docs.title: 2.1 Priority of Action
docs.text:
1. Separation
  2. Emergency handling
  3. Traffic flow

docs.title: 2.2 Controller Role
docs.text:
• Provide support and information
  • Reduce workload for the flight crew
  • Coordinate with other units

docs.title: 3. Standards
docs.text: • Safety shall not be compromised under any circumstances

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text: • Calm and clear communication is critical

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To identify and understand different emergency classifications.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• MAYDAY
  • PAN PAN
  • Urgency vs distress

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Recognise emergency declarations
  • Apply appropriate priority handling

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Emergencies are classified based on severity and urgency.

docs.title: 2.1 Distress (MAYDAY)
docs.text:
• Serious and imminent danger
  • Requires immediate assistance

docs.title: 2.2 Urgency (PAN PAN)
docs.text:
• Situation requiring attention
  • Not immediately life-threatening

docs.title: 3.1 Response
docs.text:
• Acknowledge immediately
  • Provide priority handling

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Emergency aircraft shall receive priority over all other traffic

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Delayed response increases risk

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage communication effectively during emergencies.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Priority handling
  • Frequency management
  • Clarity under pressure

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Give priority to emergency aircraft
  • Maintain clear and concise communication
  • Reduce unnecessary frequency congestion

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Effective communication is essential during emergency situations.

docs.title: 2.1 Priority
docs.text: • Emergency traffic has absolute priority

docs.title: 2.2 Frequency Control
docs.text:
• Limit non-essential transmissions
  • Maintain clear frequency

docs.title: 3.1 Communication
docs.text:
• Controllers shall use clear and direct phraseology
  • Avoid unnecessary information

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Communication shall remain clear and controlled

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Frequency congestion increases risk

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage aircraft experiencing emergencies.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Common emergency types
  • Controller support actions

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Provide assistance as requested
  • Offer suitable routing and levels
  • Coordinate with relevant units

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Aircraft emergencies may include technical failures, medical situations, or fuel issues.

docs.title: 2.1 Common Emergencies
docs.text:
• Engine failure
  • Medical emergency
  • Fuel emergency

docs.title: 2.2 Controller Actions
docs.text:
• Provide priority handling
  • Offer nearest suitable aerodrome
  • Coordinate emergency services

docs.title: 3.1 Assistance
docs.text: • Controllers shall support without overloading the crew

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Assistance shall be appropriate and timely

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Do not overwhelm pilots with unnecessary instructions

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage failures of ATC systems or aircraft equipment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Radar failure
  • Communication failure
  • Transponder failure

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Apply alternative procedures when systems fail
  • Increase separation where required

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: System failures may reduce the ability to provide radar service.

docs.title: 2.1 Radar Failure
docs.text: • Transition to procedural control if required

docs.title: 2.2 Communication Failure
docs.text: • Apply standard lost comm procedures

docs.title: 2.3 Transponder Failure
docs.text: • Identify aircraft using alternative methods

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall adapt to degraded operations

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Separation shall be maintained using appropriate methods

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • System degradation increases workload significantly

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage and recover from loss of separation.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Immediate corrective actions
  • Risk management

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Take immediate action to restore separation
  • Minimise further risk

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Loss of separation requires immediate corrective action.

docs.title: 2.1 Immediate Action
docs.text:
• Issue avoiding instructions
  • Increase separation

docs.title: 2.2 Recovery
docs.text:
• Stabilise traffic situation
  • Prevent further conflicts

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text: • Controllers shall act without delay

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Safety shall be restored as quickly as possible

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Hesitation increases severity of situation

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To recognise and manage unlawful interference situations.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Hijacking indicators
  • Communication procedures

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Recognise signs of unlawful interference
  • Follow established procedures

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Unlawful interference includes hijacking or unlawful acts onboard aircraft.

docs.title: 2.1 Indicators
docs.text:
• Unusual communication
  • Squawk 7500

docs.title: 3.1 Response
docs.text:
• Maintain communication
  • Notify appropriate authorities

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall follow established protocols

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • Do not escalate situation unnecessarily

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To introduce the principles of sectorisation and workload management within an ACC environment.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Sector structure
  • Workload factors
  • Capacity limitations

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Operate within sector limits
  • Maintain manageable workload
  • Apply effective control techniques

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Sectorisation divides airspace into manageable areas to ensure safe and efficient control.

docs.title: 2.1 Purpose
docs.text:
• Distribute workload
  • Maintain safety
  • Improve efficiency

docs.title: 3. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall not exceed manageable workload

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text: • Poor workload management leads to loss of situational awareness

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To understand how airspace is divided into sectors to ensure safe and efficient air traffic control.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Sector design principles
  • Vertical and lateral limits
  • Traffic flow alignment

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Operate within defined sector boundaries
  • Understand how sector design affects traffic flow
  • Maintain awareness of adjacent sector activity

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Sectorisation is the division of airspace into manageable units to distribute workload and maintain safe operations.

docs.title: 2.1 Sector Boundaries
docs.text:
• Defined laterally and vertically
  • Based on traffic flow and operational requirements

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Flow Alignment
docs.text:
• Sectors are designed around major traffic flows
  • Reduces crossing conflicts and coordination

docs.title: 2.3 Vertical Sectorisation
docs.text:
• Airspace may be split by level
  • Allows separation of climb, cruise, and descent traffic

docs.title: 2.4 Dynamic Sectorisation
docs.text:
• Sector boundaries may change based on traffic demand
  • Enables flexible workload management

docs.title: 3.1 Application
docs.text:
• Controllers shall remain within assigned sector limits
  • Coordination required when traffic approaches boundaries

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text:
• Sector boundaries shall be strictly observed
  • Controllers shall maintain awareness of adjacent sectors

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text:
• Poor understanding of sector boundaries leads to coordination errors
  • Sector design directly affects workload and complexity

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To manage workload effectively within an en-route sector.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Workload vs traffic count
  • Tactical vs strategic workload
  • Cognitive limits

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Maintain workload within safe limits
  • Prioritise tasks effectively
  • Anticipate workload increases

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Workload is determined by complexity, not just the number of aircraft.

docs.title: 2.1 Tactical Workload
docs.text:
• Immediate control actions
  • Conflict resolution

docs.title: 2.2 Strategic Workload
docs.text:
• Planning ahead
  • Flow management

docs.title: 2.3 Workload Drivers
docs.text:
• Crossing traffic
  • Climb/descent profiles
  • Speed differences
  • Weather

docs.title: 3.1 Workload Management
docs.text:
• Controllers shall simplify traffic where possible
  • Early action shall reduce workload

docs.title: 4. Standards
docs.text: • Controllers shall maintain situational awareness at all times

docs.title: 5. Notes
docs.text: • High workload reduces decision-making quality

docs.title: Training Objective
docs.text: To assess and manage traffic complexity within a sector.

docs.title: Key Knowledge
docs.text:
• Complexity vs traffic count
  • Conflict density
  • Traffic interaction

docs.title: Operational Application
docs.text:
Controllers shall:

  • Assess complexity continuously
  • Apply control techniques to reduce complexity
  • Avoid overload situations

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Traffic complexity reflects how difficult a traffic situation is to manage, regardless of the number of aircraft.

docs.title: 2.1 Complexity Factors
docs.text:
• Crossing traffic
  • Climb and descent profiles
  • Speed variation
  • Opposite direction traffic
  • Weather deviations

docs.title: 2.2 Conflict Density
docs.text:
• Number of potential conflicts within a sector
  • High density increases workload significantly

docs.title: 2.3 Interaction
docs.text:
• Aircraft actions affect other aircraft
  • One solution may create additional conflicts

docs.title: 2.4 Predictability
docs.text:
